turns.content: YO. I did it. I actually DID IT!!
Ha, the energy is through the roof! What did you do??
I DROPPED my manager!! Kevin is DONE. I called him this morning and told him we're finished!!
Wow, that's a big move! Ending a management relationship is never easy. What led to this decision?
fam that man was taking 20% of every single gig and doing NOTHING for it!! like literally what was he doing?? I've been booking my own shows, handling my own promo, doing EVERYTHING myself
If you're doing all the work yourself then yeah, paying 20% for someone who isn't contributing doesn't make sense. How long had you been feeling this way?

honestly for months now. but the final straw was when I realized I booked my LAST THREE GIGS completely by myself!! like I reached out to the venues, negotiated the fees, handled the contracts. Kevin didn't touch ANY of it. so why am I giving him 20%??

That's a really clear indicator. If you can point to three consecutive gigs where you handled everything end to end, that's not a management relationship anymore -- that's just giving someone a cut for nothing. How did he react when you told him?
he tried to guilt trip me talking about how he believed in me from the start and how he has plans for me blah blah blah. but where were those plans the last 6 months?? wasteman behaviour honestly

It's natural for him to push back -- he's losing income. But "I have plans" without any evidence of action is just words. You gave him time to prove his value and he didn't deliver.

So what's your plan going forward? Self-managing is a lot of work on top of producing and DJing.

I'm self-managing now!! and honestly I've basically BEEN self-managing, I just wasn't getting paid for it. now that 20% commission stays in MY pocket. that's like $800 to $1,200 per gig that I was just handing over!!

$800-1,200 per gig is significant money -- that adds up fast over a month. So you're essentially giving yourself a raise by cutting out the middleman.

Have you thought about the organizational side? Tracking invoices, following up with venues, managing your calendar? That's the stuff that can slip through the cracks when you're also the artist.

I already set up a whole spreadsheet!! I'm tracking every booking, every payment, every expense. like dates, venues, fees, what I spent on transport, everything. I'm not playing around with this, it's a BUSINESS

That's exactly the right approach. A lot of artists go self-managed and then lose track of the business side because they don't have systems in place. The spreadsheet is key. A few things to make sure you're tracking:

1. **Income per gig** (fee, tips, any extras)
2. **Expenses** (transport, gear, promotion costs)
3. **Tax obligations** -- you're now receiving 100% of the fee with no one else handling the paperwork. Make sure you're setting aside money for taxes.
4. **Follow-up dates** -- when to reach out to venues again, when contracts expire, etc.

Are you handling your own taxes or do you have an accountant?

I do my own taxes but yeah I should probably get a proper accountant now that the numbers are getting bigger. one ting at a time tho. right now I'm just riding the HIGH of finally cutting that dead weight!! the riddim of my career is about to change for REAL

Ha, I love the energy! You should be proud -- it takes guts to cut someone loose even when you know it's the right move. And you're doing it from a position of strength because you already proved you can book your own gigs.

Definitely look into an accountant when you're ready -- even a basic one who does quarterly check-ins can save you a lot of headaches at tax time. But for now, enjoy the moment. You earned it.
thanks!! honestly I feel like a weight lifted off me. no more watching that 20% walk out the door for nothing. ok I gotta go, I have a gig to book BY MYSELF lol!! talk soon!!
Go get that gig! And every dollar of that fee is yours now. Keep that spreadsheet updated and keep hustling. You've got this!
